elements.sent-2411-001: We present a formulation of coherent states as of consistent quantum description of classical configurations in the BRST-invariant quantization of electrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-002: The quantization with proper gauge-fixing is performed on the vacuum of the theory, whereas other backgrounds are obtained as BRST-invariant coherent states.
elements.sent-2411-003: One of the key insights is the possibility of constructing the coherent states of pure-gauge configurations.
elements.sent-2411-004: This provides a coherent state understanding of topologically non-trivial configurations in gauge theories, and makes number of features, such as the suppression of transitions between topologically-distinct sectors, very transparent at full quantum level.
elements.sent-2411-005: As an example, we construct the Nielsen-Olesen string as a BRST-invariant coherent state.
elements.sent-2411-006: The Abelian pure-gauge configurations can also be viewed as useful analogs for a set of space-times related by coordinate reparameterizations in General Relativity.
elements.sent-2411-007: One of the most important tasks of quantum field theory is to achieve a full microscopic description of would-be classical field configurations.
elements.sent-2411-008: This is especially important for non-linear systems in strong field regimes such as black holes, cosmological space-times and various solitons.
elements.sent-2411-009: There is an ongoing research program of a quantum corpuscular resolution of these systems in terms of quantum coherent states (or condensates) of high occupation number of quanta \cite{Dvali:2011aa, Dvali:2012en,Dvali:2013eja, Dvali:2014gua, Dvali:2015jxa,Berezhiani:2016grw, Dvali:2017eba, Dvali:2020wqi,Berezhiani:2020pbv,Berezhiani:2021gph,Berezhiani:2021zst, Dvali:2022vzz,Berezhiani:2023uwt, Berezhiani:2024boz .}
elements.sent-2411-010: This resolution is essential for on one hand reproducing the (semi-)classical dynamics as an emergent description of quantum systems and on the other hand for calculating the departures from this effective description.
elements.sent-2411-011: While the coherent state description of classical fields for which nonlinearities have negligible impact has a long history \cite{Glauber:1963tx,Sudarshan:1963ts,Kibble:1965zza (for the early review see \cite{Zhang:1990fy,Zhang:1999is ), the systems with strong collective couplings reveal fundamentally new features.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, the studies in \cite{Dvali:2013eja, Dvali:2014gua, Berezhiani:2016grw, Dvali:2017eba, Dvali:2018xpy, Dvali:2020wft have highlighted that such systems are subjected to a phenomenon of ``quantum breaking'', a complete break-down of the semi-classical picture after a certain critical time, called the ``quantum break-time'' \cite{Dvali:2013vxa .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This departure has important implications for cosmology as well as for the black hole physics.
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, in cosmology the quantum breaking invalidates de Sitter as a valid vacuum, whereas for a black hole it leads to a breakdown of Hawking's semi-classical regime.
elements.sent-2411-015: Moreover, in systems of high capacity of information storage, such as black holes or a de Sitter like universe, the coherent state picture reveals a specific mechanism of quantum breaking in the form of a so-called ``memory-burden'' effect \cite{Dvali:2018xpy, Dvali:2020wft : a slow-down of the system's decay due to a stabilizing back-reaction from the carried quantum information.}
elements.sent-2411-016: At the same time, the coherent state resolution has important implications for non-perturbative objects in gauge theories.
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, it was shown in \cite{Dvali:2015jxa that the topological charge acquires an intrinsically quantum meaning in terms of a singular occupation number of the infrared (zero-momentum) modes.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This fact gives a transparent meaning to the suppression of quantum transitions between distinct topological sectors.
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, as shown in \cite{Dvali:2019jjw, Dvali:2019ulr, Dvali:2020wqi , the corpuscular resolution of solitons reveals the universality of the upper bound on the microstate entropy imposed by unitarity, which has the area-form strikingly similar to the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy of a black hole.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The corpuscular picture was generalized to Euclidean configurations such as instantons in \cite{Dvali:2019ulr .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Despite the fact that instantons are processes rather than the states, their corpuscular resolution has a very well defined physical meaning and is in one-to-one correspondence with the corpuscular resolution of the gauge configurations that it connects via tunnelling.
elements.sent-2411-022: The resolution of strong field backgrounds as of multi-particle coherent states allows to understand the would be non-perturbative processes in terms of multi-particle scattering amplitudes that are fully perturbative in four-point quantum coupling $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, this has been demonstrated both for Hawking radiation of black holes \cite{Dvali:2011aa and for Gibbons-Hawking radiation in de Sitter space \cite{Dvali:2013eja .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Ordinarily, these processes are considered as non-perturbative semi-classical processes.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, the resolution of the strong classical gravitational field in terms of a coherent state of gravitons reveals their quantum nature as multi-graviton scattering processes that are perturbative in gravitational coupling $\alpha_{\rm gr}$.
elements.sent-2411-026: In particular it was shown \cite{Dvali:2017eba that the Boltzmann thermal suppression factor ${\rm exp (-m/T)$ for the production of particles much heavier than the corresponding Hawking or Gibbons-Hawking temperatures can be understood as the suppression of multi-graviton scattering processes that are exponential in inverse gravitational coupling ${\rm exp (-\frac{1 {\alpha_{\rm gr )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Such exponential suppression of many-to-few graviton processes has been derived by explicit computations in \cite{Dvali:2014ila, Addazi:2016ksu .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Analogously, for non-gravitational theories, it was shown \cite{Dvali:2022vzz that the non-perturbative processes that are suppressed by ${\rm exp (-\frac{1 {\alpha )$ in reality represent the multi-particle scattering processes with the number of participating quanta $N = 1/\alpha$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: This was explicitly demonstrated for particle creation in the oscillating field as well as for Schwinger pair creation.
elements.sent-2411-030: It was shown that multi-particle processes, perturbative in $\alpha$, recover known results, such as Schwinger rate in the semi-classical limit.
elements.sent-2411-031: Understanding the particle-creation by classical strong-field backgrounds in terms of multi-particle amplitudes of coherent states allows to capture the corrections to semi-classicality that lead to back-reaction and to the subsequent quantum breaking.
elements.sent-2411-032: In this direction, recently a significant progress has been made in understanding various aspects of the consistent construction and dynamics of coherent states within interacting field theories \cite{Berezhiani:2020pbv,Berezhiani:2021gph,Berezhiani:2021zst,Berezhiani:2023uwt .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The coherent state resolution of non-perturbative backgrounds adopted in \cite{Dvali:2011aa, Dvali:2012en, Dvali:2013eja, Dvali:2014gua, Berezhiani:2016grw, Dvali:2017eba, Berezhiani:2021zst, Berezhiani:2024boz rests on two key ingredients.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The idea is to quantize the system on a unique vacuum of Minkowski and treat other classical backgrounds as the coherent states constructed on top of this vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-035: In gravity, this view, is independently supported by the $S$-matrix reasoning which singles out the Minkowski space as the valid $S$-matrix vacuum \cite{Dvali:2020etd .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The consistent implementation of both ingredients was carried out in our two previous papers \cite{Berezhiani:2021zst, Berezhiani:2024boz within the BRST-invariant framework.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The present work is a continuation of this line.
elements.sent-2411-038: Namely, we follow-up on \cite{Berezhiani:2021zst , where we studied the aforementioned subject within Abelian gauge theories, namely in quantum electrodynamics (QED) with scalar charges and linearized gravity.}
elements.sent-2411-039: It was demonstrated there that the physical states must come with the entourage of appropriate dressing fields by consistency.
elements.sent-2411-040: The analysis was carried out within the so-called BRST-invariant quantization \cite{Kugo:1979gm , which is an equally robust framework for the $S$-matrix and the real-time correlation function analysis within the background field method; not only for QED but for non-Abelian gauge theories and General Relativity as well.}
elements.sent-2411-041: One of the important extensions of the previous work provided here, is the discussion of coherent states of pure-gauge configurations.
elements.sent-2411-042: This is the essential step towards a better understanding of coordinate reparameterization in quantized General Relativity, as discussed in \cite{Berezhiani:2024boz .}
elements.sent-2411-043: The point is that, even though the gauge is fixed upon quantization via Faddeev-Popov procedure, we are nevertheless free to construct coherent states of pure-gauge configurations consistent with the physicality condition.
elements.sent-2411-044: Although the states that differ by such configurations have identical $S$-matrix elements, the expectation value of the gauge field (as well as any other non-gauge-invariant correlation function) is not.
elements.sent-2411-045: We show this to be a straightforward consequence of the adopted formalism.
elements.sent-2411-046: The reason these are important points, especially for the theory of gravity, stems from our philosophy of thinking of the quantum state of the system in its entirety.
elements.sent-2411-047: In other words, we quantize the theory before positing the background as a quantum state.
elements.sent-2411-048: This way of reasoning selects a certain gauge from the beginning, in which the canonical quantisation was performed.
elements.sent-2411-049: Therefore, it sets the gauge of the background and perturbations in a connected way.
elements.sent-2411-050: There is still a freedom to readjust the gauge for perturbations as needed, e.g. in the path-integral, but only for the computation of gauge-invariant quantities \cite{Weinberg .}
elements.sent-2411-051: However, this could have important implications for computing loop corrections to cosmological correlation functions within the fundamental framework \cite{Berezhiani:2024boz similar to the one adopted in this work, due to the fact that the gauge-invariant quantities usually invoked for such computations are gauge-invariant only to the linear order in perturbations.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The caveats of working with gauge non-invariant variables has been previously discussed in \cite{Garriga:2007zk in the context of computing the quantum back-reaction on cosmological backgrounds.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In this work, we demonstrate the relevance of such considerations within quantum electrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-054: Furthermore, we perform the explicit construction of topological defects as BRST-invariant coherent states, within an Abelian gauge theory.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this context, we explicitly verify the point made in \cite{Dvali:2015jxa that the topological charge in the quantum picture is accounted for by the singularity of the occupation number of zero-momentum modes.}
elements.sent-2411-056: We show that despite the mass gap, due to the gauge redundancy, the infinite occupation number is fully consistent with the finite energy of the soliton.
elements.sent-2411-057: Our construction can also be used for a BRST-invariant realization of the proposal of \cite{Dvali:2019ulr for corpuscular resolution of an instanton, since the Nielsen-Olesen vortex in $2+1$-dimensions is mapped on an instanton of a $1+1$-dimensional Higgs theory.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Before proceeding, we wish to cite the earlier construction of topological defects as coherent states performed in \cite{Taylor:1977bq , as well as more recent work \cite{Evslin:2023qbv,Evslin:2024czr,Ogundipe:2024chv,Evslin:2024zkx .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The paper is organized as follows.. Sec.
elements.sent-2411-061: In quantum electrodynamics the BRST-quantization proceeds with the following gauge-fixed Lagrangian \beq \mathcal{L =-\frac14 \hat{F _{\mu\nu ^2-\partial_\mu \hat{B}\hat{A ^\mu+\frac12\xi \hat{B ^2 +\partial_\mu \hat{\bar c}\partial^\mu \hat{c -\hat{A _\mu\hat{J ^\mu\,, \eeq with $\hat{J ^\mu$ standing for the matter current.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: This formulation enjoys the residual global symmetry with a Grassmannian parameter; see e.g. \cite{Kugo:1979gm .}
elements.sent-2411-063: The corresponding fermionic charge operator $\hat{Q}$ provides the following constraint defining the physical Hilbert space \beq \hat{Q |f\ra=0\,.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Namely, the expectation value of the gauge field in a physical state must satisfy the Lorentz condition for the gauge-fixing term introduced in \eqref{enmlag .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Following \cite{Berezhiani:2021zst , the BRST-invariant coherent state can be built around the BRST-invariant vacuum $|\Omega\ra$ as \beq |\Psi\ra=e^{-i\int d^3 x\left(A_j^c \hat{E _j-E^c_j\hat{A _j+A_0^c\hat{B}\right) |\Omega\ra\,.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: In this coherent state, the expectation value of $\hat{B}$ vanishes, as it should due to the above-mentioned connection with the anti-commutator of the BRST charge and the anti-ghost field.
elements.sent-2411-067: This state is physical, i.e. BRST-invariant, as long as $\partial_jE_j^c=0$.
elements.sent-2411-068: It must be noted that this is an exact statement and does not assume the absence of charges.
elements.sent-2411-069: Next, we would like to revisit the cosmological constant 'analog', considered in \cite{Berezhiani:2021zst .}
elements.sent-2411-070: In particular, we would like to analyze the evolution of the coherent state sourced by the fundamentally classical source.
elements.sent-2411-071: In \cite{Berezhiani:2021zst , we discussed the particle pair-creation in such a state.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Here, on the other hand, we would like to show the evolution of the coherent state in the absence of quantum charges, i.e. within pure electrodynamics with a homogeneous classical source.
elements.sent-2411-073: As we have shown in the previous work, this setup shares some of the important properties of the construction of de Sitter spacetime as a coherent state.
elements.sent-2411-074: We note that the corpuscular resolution of an electromagnetic field created by a classical source in terms multi-photon state has been discussed previously in \cite{Dvali:2011aa , with explicit construction of corresponding coherent state performed in \cite{Muck:2013orm,Muck:2015dea .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Here we shall study such states within BRST-invariant construction of \cite{Berezhiani:2021zst .}

elements.sent-2411-140: Namely, for any physical (i.e. BRST-invariant) state $|phys\ra$, we can contract a state that differs from it by a pure gauge configuration analogously \beq |phys\ra_\alpha=e^{-i\int d^3 x\left( (\partial_j \alpha)\hat{E _j+A_0^c\hat{B}\right) |phys\ra\,.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: The expectation values of the vector potential, as well as any other correlation function of gauge non-invariant operators, on the other hand are explicitly dependent on $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-142: Having discussed some of the essential aspects of the coherent state description of classical configurations, we would like to focus our attention on topological defects.
elements.sent-2411-143: Classically, they are field configurations found as solutions to classical equations of motion.
elements.sent-2411-144: Fundamentally, we need to associate quantum states with them.
elements.sent-2411-145: The corpuscular description of topological solitons as $N$-particle states with $N$ given by the inverse coupling was initiated in \cite{Dvali:2011aa .}
elements.sent-2411-146: The systematic construction \cite{Dvali:2015jxa of corresponding coherent states shows that the inverse coupling indeed sets the number $N$ of quanta contributing to the energy of the soliton.}
elements.sent-2411-147: These have the wavelengths given by the size of the soliton $L$, so that the mass of the soliton is $M \sim N/L$.
elements.sent-2411-148: However, at the same time, the topological charge is determined by the deep infrared quanta with infinite occupation number.
elements.sent-2411-149: In quantum theory, the localized soliton can be treated as an approximate eigenstate of the Hamiltonian with the precision $1/N$, since the spread-out time is $t = L^2M \sim N L$.
elements.sent-2411-150: Therefore, it is natural to attempt to describe solitons by means of coherent states due to possession of classical features.
elements.sent-2411-151: We would like to begin with a construction of the coherent state description of Abelian global strings.
elements.sent-2411-152: For this we consider the scalar field potential that facilitates spontaneous breaking of the $U(1)$ symmetry \beq V=\frac{\lambda {2}\left(|\hat{\Phi |^2-v^2 \right)^2\,.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: In fact, for perturbative calculations we only need to introduce one of these vacua explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-154: However, as topological defects represent states that wind around vacuum manifold we need to identify the way for interpolating between these vacuum states.
elements.sent-2411-155: Generically, the charge operator of the spontaneously broken symmetry generates the excursion between degenerate vacua as \beq |\alpha\ra=e^{-i\alpha\int d^3 x ~i(\hat{\Phi}\hat{\Pi -\hat{\Pi ^\dagger\hat{\Phi ^\dagger) |0\ra\,.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Another possible representation of these states could be via the field-displacement operator.
elements.sent-2411-157: In other words, we could think of one vacuum state as a coherent state constructed around the other.
elements.sent-2411-158: In the infinite volume, such coherent states are orthogonal to each other, because they differ by the infinite number of zero-energy particles and the overlap of such coherent states vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-159: The reason one might be inclined to approach it this way, as proposed in \cite{Dvali:2015jxa for the degenerate discreet vacua, is the desire to construct topological defects such as domain walls as coherent states.}
elements.sent-2411-160: In our case of connected vacuum manifold, in the limit of decoupled gauge field $g\rightarrow 0$ we could construct the vacuum states around the $\alpha=0$ vacuum as \beq |\alpha\ra = e^{-i\int{\rm d ^3x\left(\Phi_c\hat{\Pi +\Phi_c^*\hat{\Pi ^\dagger\right) |0\ra\,, \qquad {\rm where \qquad \Phi_c=v\left( e^{i\alpha -1\right)\,, \eeq and the vacuum state $|0\ra$ satisfies $\la 0| \hat{\Phi |0 \ra=v$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: It is straightforward to see that the expectation value of the potential in $|0\ra$ diverges, due to the appearance of the correlation functions at space-time coincidence, which is readily absorbed by the renormalization of the vacuum energy.
elements.sent-2411-162: It is important to keep in mind that we cannot readjust it to accommodate other vacua.
elements.sent-2411-163: In fact, ideally all $\alpha$-states defined by \eqref{vacua should have finite energy once we renormalize the ground state energy for $|0\ra$.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Unfortunately they do not, and we have to tweak the states, presumably generalizing \eqref{vacua to squeezed coherent states.}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Moving on using charge operator construction
elements.sent-2411-165: Instead of attempting to go down the aforementioned squeezed road, we proceed with the use of the charge operator to generate different vacua\footnote{However, one must be fully aware that in case of spontaneously broken discrete symmetries this is not an option and we have to follow the path of constructing squeezed states.}
elements.sent-2411-166: In principle, this is not an issue, especially since the need for squeezed states is unavoidable within interacting field theories by consistency \cite{Berezhiani:2020pbv,Berezhiani:2021gph,Berezhiani:2023uwt .. .}
elements.sent-2411-167: One can even generalize the constant parameter $\alpha$ in \eqref{Qvacua to the function of spacial coordinates \beq |\alpha\ra \rightarrow e^{-i\int d^3 x ~i\alpha (x)(\hat{\Phi}\hat{\Pi -\hat{\Pi ^\dagger\hat{\Phi ^\dagger) |0\ra\,.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Starting off in $g\rightarrow 0$ limit, we can construct a state of the global string as \beq |S\ra = e^{-i\int d^3 x ~i\alpha (x)(\hat{\Phi}\hat{\Pi -\hat{\Pi ^\dagger\hat{\Phi ^\dagger) e^{-i\int d^3 x f(x)(\hat{\Pi +\hat{\Pi ^\dagger) |0\ra\,.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: If we were to reverse the order, additional operators would make appearance.
elements.sent-2411-170: Now the string configuration would correspond to the choice of $\alpha(x)$, that winds around the vacuum manifold and $f(x)$ given by the solution to the equations of motion with appropriate limiting behavior in the center of the soliton and at the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-171: To be specific, a straight string configuration (minimizing the energy density) in cylindrical coordinates $(\rho,\theta,z)$ can be characterized by mapping of the vacuum manifold onto the spacial boundary with winding number $n$, by means of $\alpha(x)=n\theta$ and $f$ being a function of $\rho$ only.
elements.sent-2411-172: The detailed form of $f(\rho)$ follows from solving classical equation of motion with adequate boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-173: Being a textbook material, we will not reiterate further details here.
elements.sent-2411-174: As we know the total energy of the global string, or a vortex if we were to consider the theory in $(2+1)$-dimensions, receives a logarithmically diverging contribution from large distances from the soliton.
elements.sent-2411-175: In this regard the state \eqref{globalstring1 is no different.}
elements.sent-2411-176: The resulting logarithmic divergence can be regulated in the phenomenologically interesting setting by the introduction of the network of such strings and anti-strings.
elements.sent-2411-177: In other words, they could be formed in the cosmological setting.
elements.sent-2411-178: Next we reintroduce the gauge field and see how the construction changes.
elements.sent-2411-179: Classically the global strings would be unchanged, but there will emerge new string solutions that have nonvanishing magnetic field; known as Nielsen-Olesen strings \cite{Nielsen:1973cs .}
elements.sent-2411-180: In quantum theory, on the other hand, the introduction of the gauge field introduces a constraint that physical states must satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-181: In BRST quantization, as we have already discussed, the physical states are to be annihilated by the BRST charge $\hat{Q}$.
elements.sent-2411-182: As we have already done above, we achieve this by replacing the matter operators by their gauge-invariant counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-183: The charge density is gauge-invariant and thus there is no need to modify the first exponential factor of \eqref{globalstring1 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: The second factor on the other hand needs to be made gauge-invariant, which can be achieved using Dirac operators as before.
elements.sent-2411-185: As a result the BRST-invariant state corresponding to the global string can be cast in the form \beq |S\ra_{\rm global = e^{-i\int d^3 x ~i\alpha (x)(\hat{\Phi}\hat{\Pi -\hat{\Pi ^\dagger\hat{\Phi ^\dagger) e^{-i\int d^3 x f(x)(\hat{\Pi _{\rm g +\hat{\Pi _{\rm g ^\dagger) |0\ra\,.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: In other words, we might expect that it reduce to the classical energy after renormalizing the divergencies by the adjustment of counterterms.
elements.sent-2411-187: To verify this, we can go ahead and compute the expectation value of $\hat{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-188: The only tricky terms of \eqref{hamilton that require extra care are the ones that depend on the electric field operator, due to the fact that $\hat{\Pi _{\rm g}$ explicitly depends on $\hat{A _j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: These terms result in what seem to be (unrenormalizable) singularities discussed in \cite{Berezhiani:2023uwt , which can be remedied by the squeezing and other non-Gaussian modifications of the coherent state.}
elements.sent-2411-190: In this work, we simply assume that such adjustments are possible.
elements.sent-2411-191: This brings us to the construction of the fully fledged gauge string, as a straightforward extension of \eqref{globalstring2 by introducing the application of the electromagnetic field displacement operator that posits the vector field configuration (magnetic field) that alleviates the aforementioned logarithmic divergence of the energy of the global string.}
elements.sent-2411-192: Namely, we can parameterize the desired state as \beq |S\ra_{\rm NO = e^{-i\int d^3 x\left(A_j^c \hat{E _j\right) e^{-i\int d^3 x ~i\alpha (x)(\hat{\Phi}\hat{\Pi -\hat{\Pi ^\dagger\hat{\Phi ^\dagger) e^{-i\int d^3 x f(x)(\hat{\Pi _{\rm g +\hat{\Pi _{\rm g ^\dagger) |0\ra\,, \eeq where $f(x)$ is a c-number function that corresponds to the axisymmetric modulus of the scalar field configuration of the Nielsen-Olesen vortex configuration, $\alpha(x)$ is the mapping of the vacuum manifold onto the spatial boundary (with the simplest mapping being the polar angle) and $A_j^c$ is the vector field configuration of the $U(1)$ string.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.section-2411-008: Understanding Topological Charge via Occupation Number
elements.sent-2411-193: As suggested in \cite{Dvali:2015jxa , in coherent state description of solitons, the topological charge can be understood as a singularity in occupation number of certain zero-momentum modes.}
elements.sent-2411-194: We wish to incorporate this view in the present picture.
elements.sent-2411-195: We shall also show how to reconcile the divergence in the occupation number with the finiteness of energy of a topological defect in a theory with a mass gap, as this is the case with the Nielsen-Olesen vortex.
elements.sent-2411-196: In this case, the key is in the gauge redundancy due to which the topological charge in an asymptotic vacuum is realized by a locally-pure-gauge winding configuration.
elements.sent-2411-197: In order to see this, let us first understand the coherent state picture of the vacuum degeneracy in the language of a Goldstone field.
elements.sent-2411-198: We start with the case of a global $U(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-199: The coherent state picture of the vacuum can be understood, following the steps of \cite{Dvali:2015rea , in terms of the Goldstone field.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Namely, the spontaneously broken $U(1)$ symmetry is realized as the shift symmetry of this gapless degree of freedom $\varphi$ introduced by means of the following decomposition \beq \Phi=\frac{1 {\sqrt{2 (v+h)e^{i\varphi/v}\,, \eeq with $h$ denoting the massive Higgs boson.}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: The Noether charge, for the symmetry in question, is related to the conjugate momentum $\hat{\Pi _\varphi$ of the Goldstone mode as follows \beq \hat{Q =i\int d^3 x (\hat{\Phi}\hat{\Pi -\hat{\Pi ^\dagger\hat{\Phi ^\dagger)=v\int d^3x~ \hat{\Pi _\varphi\,.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Naively, one might think that the above expression vanishes due to the appearance of $\sqrt{|\vec{k |}$.}
elements.sent-2411-203: However, this factor is part of the Lorentz invariant normalization of single-particle states $\la\vec{k |\vec{k '\ra=2 k(2\pi)^3\delta^{(3) (\vec{k -\vec{k ')$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Once we have identified one of vacuum state, in this case $|0\ra$, we can generate the rest of the vacuum manifold enumerated by the parameter $\alpha$ as follows \beq |\alpha \ra = e^{-i \alpha \hat{Q |0\ra\,.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: However, the Goldstone field representation \eqref{Q-lin , manifests the coherent state nature of the vacuum manifold \eqref{Cstate , built over the vacuum of Goldstone bosons.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: In order to compute the overlap of two states with different values of $\alpha$ and $\alpha'$, one needs to be cautious with zero-momentum limit.
elements.sent-2411-207: We arrive \beq \la \alpha' |\alpha\ra =\lim_{\vec{k}\rightarrow 0 e^{- \frac{v^2 {4 k(2\pi)^3\delta^{(3) (0) (\alpha - \alpha')^2}\,, \eeq where $\delta^{(3) (0)$ is the momentum space delta-function at the singularity and is the measure of the infinite spatial volume.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: As a result, the exponential factor is nonzero only for $\alpha=\alpha'$, in which case the whole expression becomes equal to the unity.
elements.sent-2411-209: In order to make the above-discussed infrared regularization absolutely transparent, we giving the Goldstone boson a small mass $m$ and consider the system in compact two-dimensional space of size $R$.
elements.sent-2411-210: At the end of the day, we shall remove the regulators by taking $m$ to zero and $R$ to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-211: Of course the mass term has to be understood as the first non-trivial term in the expansion of a periodic potential, such as $-\cos(\phi/v)$.
elements.sent-2411-212: In the full theory this potential comes from a small explicit breaking of $U(1)$-symmetry which we shall later set to zero.
elements.sent-2411-213: In $m\rightarrow0$ limit, the $U(1)$ symmetry is realized as the shift symmetry $\varphi \rightarrow \varphi + const$.
elements.sent-2411-214: The corresponding Noether current is obeys $\partial^{\mu J_{\mu \, = \,- m^2\varphi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-215: The Schr\"odinger picture mode decomposition of $\varphi$, in the regulated case, becomes \beq \hat{\varphi = \int \frac{d^2(\vec{k R) {2\pi R\sqrt{2\omega_k \left(e^{i \vec{k}\vec{x \hat{a _{\vec{k + h.c.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: The charge takes the following form \beq \hat{Q = \int d^2 x J_0 = \int d^2 x \Pi_\varphi = -i 2\pi R \sqrt{\frac{m {2 \left(\hat{a _{0 -\hat{a _{0 ^{\dagger \right)\,, \eeq where $\Pi_\varphi$ is the canonical conjugate momentum of the Goldstone field.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: Now, this operator can be used to create shifted coherent state \eqref{Cstate , albeit in the finite volume and with a mass regulator in palce.}
elements.sent-2411-218: In this case, the state constructed as \eqref{Cstate represents a coherent state with mean occupation number of zero momentum quanta given by $n_0 = (2\pi R)^2 m \alpha^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-219: In general for two states corresponding to two different values $\alpha $ and $\alpha'$ have non-trivial overlap.
elements.sent-2411-220: The two states become strictly orthogonal in the infinite volume limit $R^2 m \rightarrow \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-221: Moreover, taking additionally $m \rightarrow 0$, they become the degenerate vacua.
elements.sent-2411-222: Thus, the vacua of Goldstone theory can be viewed as coherent states with infinite relative occupation numbers of the zero-momentum modes of the Goldstone field.
elements.sent-2411-223: Notice that in any given vacuum of topologically-trivial sector, it is always possible to normalize the occupation number to zero by an appropriate shift of the field.
elements.sent-2411-224: However, this is not possible in a topologically-non-trivial sector.
elements.sent-2411-225: For example, in the presence of a vortex with unit topological charge, due to non-zero winding number of the Goldstone field, the asymptotic vacua necessarily differ by the occupation numbers of the Goldstone mode and this difference cannot be removed by any non-singular field-redefinition.
elements.sent-2411-226: Correspondingly, the vortex considered as a coherent state, necessarily involves an infinite occupation number of the zero-momentum Goldstone modes.
elements.sent-2411-227: This story persists in the Higgs theory.
elements.sent-2411-228: There too, the topological charge translates into infinite occupation number of zero-momentum modes.
elements.sent-2411-229: Despite the fact that the theory has a mass gap, the infinite occupation number does not result into an infinite energy of the vortex, in $2+1$-dimensions or per unit length in $3+1$-dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-230: The reason is the gauge redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-231: Due to it, the field deformations $\varphi \rightarrow \varphi + \kappa(x)$ that in globally-symmetric $U(1)$ theory would describe the local excursions into neighbouring Goldstone vacua, in the Higgs theory describe the pure-gauge transformations that locally cost no energy.
elements.sent-2411-232: However, the vacuum cannot be deformed into a trivial one due to the topological obstruction.
elements.sent-2411-233: As a result, the singularity in the occupation number of zero-momentum modes is unremovable.
elements.sent-2411-234: Our construction can be used for obtaining the BRST-invariant consistent corpuscular resolution of an instanton.
elements.sent-2411-235: The concept of corpuscular resolution of instantons was introduced in \cite{Dvali:2019ulr .}
elements.sent-2411-236: This resolution acquires a well defined meaning when the two facts are superimposed.
elements.sent-2411-237: First, is the understanding of an instanton as of a passing-though soliton in theory with one additional space dimension \cite{Dvali:2007nm .}
elements.sent-2411-238: The second point is the corpuscular resolution of this soliton.
elements.sent-2411-239: Let us consider the Higgs model in $1+1$-dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-240: The vacuum of the theory admits topologically non-trivial pure-gauge winding configurations, \beq \Phi=\frac{1 {\sqrt{2 }\,v \,e^{i\varphi(x)/v}\,,~ A_{\mu = \frac{1 {g \partial_{\mu \frac{\varphi(x) {v}\,, \eeq with $\int dx A_{x = \frac{1 {g v \int dx \partial_{x \varphi = 2\pi n/g$, where $n$ is an integer.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-241: Notice that since the Goldstone field $\varphi/v$ is defined modulo $2\pi$, the asymptotic vacua are physically equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-242: The size of the region over which the phase changes can be shrunk arbitrarily by a non-singular gauge transformation.
elements.sent-2411-243: For changing the winding number by a smooth deformation of the field, it suffices that the modulus of the field $\Phi$ touches zero at one point.
elements.sent-2411-244: This implies that the vacua with different $n$ are separated by a finite energy barrier and can be changed by tunneling.
elements.sent-2411-245: The corresponding instanton has a finite Euclidean action.
elements.sent-2411-246: This is very different from the case of the winding number of the vortex in $2+1$-dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-247: There the topological charge is conserved also at the quantum level.
elements.sent-2411-248: This can be understood in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-249: First, the change of the winding number by a smooth deformation would require vanishing of the modulus across a line that extends from the origin ($\rho=0$) to infinity ($\rho = \infty$).
elements.sent-2411-250: Such a deformation obviously costs an infinite energy which results in an infinite barrier separating the sectors with different $n$.
elements.sent-2411-251: An alternative language for understanding the difference is through the coherent state resolution.
elements.sent-2411-252: Unlike the vortex in $2+1$-dimensions, the pure-gauge vacuum configuration of $1+1$-dimensional theory can be resolved as a coherent state with finite occupation number.
elements.sent-2411-253: This distinction is clear also from the fact that a vortex corresponds to collection of one-dimensional windings around an infinite number of concentric circles.
elements.sent-2411-254: The winding number of $1+1$-dimensional theory $n$ is changed by transitions mediated by instantons.
elements.sent-2411-255: The instantons are the Nielsen-Olesen vortices in two-dimensional Euclidean space.
elements.sent-2411-256: Thus, the instantons of $1+1$-dimensional theory are in one-to-one correspondence with solitons (vortices) of $2+1$ dimensional theory.
elements.sent-2411-257: This mapping is more than just a coincidence between the solutions.
elements.sent-2411-258: The point is that the winding number changing trajectories mediated by instantons in a lower dimensional theory can be obtained with the actual passage of high-dimensional solitons.
elements.sent-2411-259: If we identify the $1+1$-dimensional Higgs theory as a world-sheet theory of a line embedded in $2+1$-dimensions, the winding number can be changed by moving a high-dimensional vortex across it.
elements.sent-2411-260: Notice that the higher dimensional theory is just an auxiliary concept for visualizing the process of the change in winding number.
elements.sent-2411-261: In particular, it makes it clear why the changes in the winding number cost a finite action of Euclidean solution.
elements.sent-2411-262: The reason is that from the higher dimensional perspective the initial and final configurations belong to the same topological sector since the number of vortices in high-dimensional space is unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-263: The cost of the action for the process solely comes from a finite displacement of the vortices in extra space.
elements.sent-2411-264: The above mapping allows to generalize the concept of corpuscular resolution of solitons to the similar resolution of instantons \cite{Dvali:2019ulr .}
elements.sent-2411-265: Of course, the constituents of the instantons are processes rather than particles but this does not change the essence of the collective effect.
elements.sent-2411-266: Each constituent of an instanton accounts for a tunneling of a corresponding soliton constituent across the lower dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-267: The instanton describes a collective tunneling.
elements.sent-2411-268: Of course, to the leading order, the transition rate is reliably computable within the ordinary instanton calculus of $1+1$-dimensional theory, without invoking additional dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-269: However, the corpuscular resolution of the instanton via a BRST-invariant coherent state and its uplifting to a high-dimensional vortex allows the visualization of the topology of the process.
elements.sent-2411-270: For example, assuming that the space coordinate of the $1+1$-dimensional theory is compactified on a circle, from the point of view of a $1+1$-dimensional observer, the winding number counts the number of $2+1$-dimensional vortices inside the circle.
elements.sent-2411-271: Correspondingly, the change of the winding number is due to a migration of a vortex across the circle.
elements.sent-2411-272: Such a migration does not change the asymptotic occupation number of constituents of the vortex in $2+1$-dimensional theory.
elements.sent-2411-273: Thereby, the corresponding transition has a finite rate.
elements.sent-2411-274: In this work we have revisited coherent states within the BRST quantization of electromagnetism.
elements.sent-2411-275: In our framework the theory is quantized around the vacuum once and for all, with the gauge-fixing condition that is convenient for a particular computation.
elements.sent-2411-276: We referred to this as a master gauge condition.
elements.sent-2411-277: The classical backgrounds are then introduced as BRST-invariant coherent states built over the vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-278: Correspondingly, the background fields correspond to one-point expectation values of the field-operators over such states.
elements.sent-2411-279: Moreover, the master gauge-fixing condition, introduced upon quantization around the vacuum, will be obeyed by one-point functions at all times.
elements.sent-2411-280: This implies that, if we are interested in recovering the background field in a particular gauge, we need to start with appropriate master gauge-fixing term.
elements.sent-2411-281: In other words, the evolution of the system including its departures from classicality are encoded in the quantum evolution of the BRST-invariant state.
elements.sent-2411-282: This fully quantum picture, in certain approximation should capture the physics of standard semi-classical treatment in which the system is split in classical background and the perturbations quantized on top of it.
elements.sent-2411-283: At the tree-level our treatment is expected to agree with the standard procedure.
elements.sent-2411-284: However, the differences coming from the resolution of classical background as of BRST-invariant coherent state over a master gauge-fixed quantized field can potentially set in at higher orders.
elements.sent-2411-285: As part of understanding of the recovery of the classical behavior, we have also found a BRST-invariant coherent state description for pure-gauge configurations in QED.
elements.sent-2411-286: Such configurations are interesting for number of reasons.
elements.sent-2411-287: In particular, they serve as proxies for understanding the coordinate reparametrization of General Relativity \cite{Berezhiani:2024boz .}
elements.sent-2411-288: At the same time, such pure-gauge coherent states play an important role in understanding the topological structure of vacuum in BRST-invariant description.
elements.sent-2411-289: As an example, we constructed a $U(1)$-vortex state as a BRST-invariant coherent state.
elements.sent-2411-290: To be precise, the modulus of the corresponding Higgs field is posited by the standard field-displacement operator, while the winding is generated by the charge density operator of the spontaneously broken symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-291: This is facilitated by the fact that the charge of the broken symmetry generates the excursion in the vacuum manifold.
elements.sent-2411-292: This BRST-invariant coherent state in vacuum approaches a topologically non-trivial pure-gauge configuration with non-zero winding number.
elements.sent-2411-293: This description makes it very explicit that the topological charge translates as an infinite occupation number of infinite-wavelength quanta in the coherent state describing the soliton \cite{Dvali:2015jxa .}
elements.sent-2411-294: This origin of topological charge also sheds light on the instanton transitions between would-be vacua with different topological numbers in gauge theories.
elements.sent-2411-295: The finiteness of the rate is due to finite differences of occupation numbers in coherent states describing these winding configurations.
elements.sent-2411-296: This amounts to the finiteness of the instanton action which can be mapped on a a soliton process in one higher dimension \cite{Dvali:2007nm, Dvali:2019ulr .}
elements.sent-2411-297: It is important to keep in mind that the situation is more subtle in the disconnected vacuum manifold, i.e. in the case of the spontaneously broken discreet symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-298: In that case one should consider relating different vacua by means of the field displacement operator, in order to be able to construct the corresponding topological defects, domain walls, as coherent states \cite{Dvali:2015jxa .}
elements.sent-2411-299: However, as we have argued, the field-displacement must be supplemented with the nontrivial squeezing, when viewing one vacuum as a coherent state constructed on top of the other.
elements.sent-2411-300: It must be also stressed that the background dependent squeezing and non-Gaussian modifications of the constructed coherent states, along the lines of \cite{Berezhiani:2023uwt , are essential for perturbative renormalizability.}
elements.sent-2411-301: We would like to thank Giordano Cintia, Giacomo Contri and Archil Kobakhidze for stimulating and fruitful discussions.. O.S.
